Scenario: Sneha works in Vietnamese while Ravi views the same workspace in English and both open the same bug details and comments during the same time window.
Test Conditions: Language preference must be isolated per user while shared workspace content remains consistent and user-entered text remains unaltered.
Test Idea: Sneha, a support manager in Pune, prefers Vietnamese because she is coordinating with a pilot customer. Ravi, an engineering manager in Hyderabad, stays on English. Both open the same Bugasura workspace and the same bug record at nearly the same time. Sneha reads menus, buttons, dialogs, and system messages in Vietnamese, while Ravi expects English. The bug title and comments were written by users and include Vietnamese diacritics. This journey crosses per-user language preference, mixed-language workspace behavior, localization boundaries, and the rule that user-entered content must remain unchanged. What breaks if this fails: when Sneha changes her language, Ravi’s view also changes, or the bug title/comment text is incorrectly translated or corrupted. That means the shared workspace and shared entity handling between user preference and content rendering has failed. It is a classic seam issue: the same data object is consumed by two stories with different presentation rules.
Acceptance Criteria: Sneha sees product-owned UI text in Vietnamese while Ravi sees product-owned UI text in English in the same workspace., Neither user’s language setting changes the other user’s interface., Bug titles, comments, and other user-entered content remain exactly as entered for both users., Vietnamese diacritics render correctly in shared content for both users., Shared actions on the same workspace do not overwrite per-user language state.

Scenario: Arjun switches Bugasura to Vietnamese and continues working through navigation, dialogs, settings, and API-backed screens in the same session without refreshing the page.
Test Conditions: A language change in settings must propagate immediately and consistently to connected UI modules and localized API-backed content within the same session.
Test Idea: Arjun is a product analyst in Bengaluru helping a Vietnamese-speaking client during a live demo. He starts in English, opens profile settings, chooses Vietnamese, and immediately continues to the dashboard, bug list, filters, detail dialogs, and settings pages. This journey crosses language selection, immediate UI refresh behavior, localization of product-owned text, and API responses designed to return localized strings. What breaks if this fails: Arjun changes the language successfully in settings, but downstream screens continue to show stale English labels, partially translated dialogs, or API-backed messages in the old language. That means the settings story works in isolation, but the handoff to dependent modules and localized responses fails, leaving a fragmented mixed-state experience during a real workflow. The requirements explicitly call for immediate-feeling switching, localized UI surfaces, and localized API-backed text where designed.
Acceptance Criteria: After selecting Vietnamese, the UI updates within the same interaction without forced full-page refresh., Navigation items, menus, settings labels, dialogs, and common error messages appear in Vietnamese., API responses designed to return localized text are rendered in Vietnamese in the same session., No broken placeholders, untranslated keys, or stale English strings remain on in-scope surfaces except permitted fallback cases., The workflow stays functionally usable while language text changes.
Test Steps:
Step 1: Launch Bugasura in English language. 
Step 2: Navigate to 'Profile Settings'. 
Step 3: Select 'Vietnamese' from the language dropdown. 
Step 4: Click 'Save Changes' button. 
Step 5: Verify UI updates to Vietnamese without page refresh. 
Step 6: Navigate to 'Dashboard', 'Bug List', and 'Settings'. 
Step 7: Verify all navigation items and dialogs appear in Vietnamese. 
Step 8: Verify API-backed messages are localized to Vietnamese. 
Step 9: Verify no stale English strings remain.

Scenario: Priya opens Bugasura for the first time in Vietnamese, changes her language to English in settings, signs out, and later signs in from another device where the browser is still set to Vietnamese.
Test Conditions: Explicitly saved language preference on the server must override browser/IP auto-detection across logout, login, browser change, and device change.
Test Idea: Priya is a QA lead in Chennai working with a Vietnamese customer pilot. On her first visit, Bugasura detects Vietnamese from her browser or location and opens the product in Vietnamese. She then goes into profile settings and explicitly changes her language to English because she is preparing English screenshots for her internal team. Later that day, she logs out and signs in again from another laptop whose browser is still configured for Vietnamese. This journey crosses automatic language detection, explicit user language selection, server-side persistence, and cross-device restoration of preference. What breaks if this fails: Priya’s saved English preference is lost at the handoff between detection logic and persisted profile preference, so the second device reverts her back to Vietnamese. That creates a seam failure where first-load detection overrides the already-saved user choice, making the product feel unreliable and causing confusion in a core onboarding-to-settings-to-login workflow. This flow is grounded in the requirement that saved user preference must override automatic detection and persist across sessions and devices.
Acceptance Criteria: On first load with no saved preference, Bugasura may default to Vietnamese based on detection., After Priya changes language to English, the language preference is saved in her user profile as the authoritative source., After logout and login on another device with Vietnamese browser settings, the application opens in English, not Vietnamese., No stale Vietnamese labels remain after login on the second device., The workflow remains usable without requiring manual reset of language preference.
Test Steps:
Step 1: Launch Bugasura with browser language set to Vietnamese. 
Step 2: Verify UI labels, navigation, and menu items appear in Vietnamese. 
Step 3: Navigate to 'Profile Settings'. 
Step 4: Select 'English' from the language dropdown. 
Step 5: Click 'Save Changes' button. 
Step 6: Verify language preference is saved in user profile. 
Step 7: Sign out of Bugasura. 
Step 8: Sign in on another device where browser language is set to Vietnamese. 
Step 9: Verify application opens in English. 
Step 10: Verify no stale Vietnamese labels remain.
